section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT

paragraphs.text: The views expressed herein are being presented on behalf of the Sections of Antitrust and International Law. They have not been reviewed or approved by the House of Delegates or the Board of Governors of the American Bar Association and, accordingly, should not be construed as representing the position of the Association Bar Association.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT

paragraphs.text: The Antitrust Law Section and International Law Section of the American Bar Association (the Sections) welcome the opportunity to comment on the European Commission's (the Commission's) consultation regarding an impact assessment for the Digital Fairness Act (the Consultation). The Consultation seeks input on potential improvements in EU consumer law to strengthen the protection of consumers in general and includes a questionnaire (the Questionnaire). The Sections commend the Commission for conducting the Consultation and are grateful for the opportunity to respond to the Questionnaire.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT

paragraphs.text: The Antitrust Law Section (ALS) is the world's largest professional organization for antitrust and competition law, trade regulation, consumer protection and data privacy as well as related aspects of economics. Section members, numbering over 11,000, come from all over the world and include attorneys and non-lawyers from private law firms, in-house counsel, non-profit organizations, consulting firms, federal and state government agencies, as well as judges, professors, and law students. The Section provides a broad variety of programs and publications concerning all facets of antitrust and the other listed fields. Numerous ALS members have extensive experience and expertise regarding similar laws of non-U.S. jurisdictions. For more than thirty years, ALS has been honored to provide input to enforcement agencies around the world conducting consultations on topics within the ALS's scope of expertise.[2]

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT

paragraphs.text: The International Law Section (ILS) is the American Bar Association section that focuses on international legal issues, the promotion of the rule of law, and the provision of legal education, policy, publishing and practical assistance related to cross-border activity. Its members total over 11,000, including private practitioners, in-house counsel, attorneys in governmental and inter-government entities, and legal academics, and represent over 100 countries. The ILS's more than 50 substantive committees cover competition law, trade law, and data privacy and data security

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT

paragraphs.text: law worldwide as well as areas of law, which often intersect with these areas, such as mergers and acquisitions and joint ventures. Throughout its century of existence, the ILS has provided input to debates relating to international legal policy. With respect to competition law and policy specifically, the ILS has provided input for decades to authorities around the world.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT

paragraphs.text: These comments reflect the expertise and experience of the Sections’ members with consumer protection laws, competition laws and enforcement practices around the world. The Sections are available to provide additional comments, or otherwise to assist the Commission as it may deem appropriate.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT

paragraphs.text: The Sections commend the Commission’s proposal to undertake a thorough review of its laws and enforcement to protect consumers in the digital age, including through thoughtful engagement with consumers and other stakeholders alike.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT

evidence_quotes.quote: The Questionnaire defines dark patterns as “the design of digital interfaces that can influence consumers to take decisions they would not have taken otherwise.”
“particularly when the offer is relevant to the individual consumer.”

paragraphs.text: The Questionnaire defines dark patterns as “the design of digital interfaces that can influence consumers to take decisions they would not have taken otherwise.”⁴ The Sections agree that some such practices constitute unfair practices. However, there is a significant difference between knowing deception to induce consumers to make choices that only harm them and steering consumers to select an option that is beneficial to the marketer but may also have consumer benefits (e.g., a greater discount). Marketers and resellers have long worked to influence consumer choice, and in most cases, there is nothing nefarious about such practices, including offering a pop up to advertise a discount when purchasing multiple products or when signing up to receive marketing texts, particularly when the offer is relevant to the individual consumer.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
SPECIFIC COMMENTS AND RECOMMENDATIONS
I. Section 1: Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: As regards the practices for which the Commission is seeking comment, e.g., click fatigue, nagging, pressuring, confirm-shaming or presenting certain choices in a leading manner, the Sections note that persuasion in and of itself would not be unfair unless there is also a showing of deception and consumer harm. The Sections believe that understanding what constitutes a dark pattern is still developing and recommend that the Commission use caution against labelling as unfair design choices that may simplify a user’s experience and provide a consumer benefit. We encourage the Commission to continue discussion with experts and stakeholders to better understand what user interfaces qualify as dark patterns that are clearly unfair to all reasonable consumers, and to focus its efforts on eliminating these practices, without deterring design choices that simply encourage consumers to make certain decisions. In the U.S., a practice is unfair if it

paragraphs.text: Among unfair practices is the use of dark patterns to mislead individuals into giving up privacy, e.g., through hiding privacy controls or fatiguing or confusing users into giving consent to process their personal data. The FTC’s long history of privacy enforcement against dark patterns was discussed in the FTC Staff Report, *Bringing Dark Patterns to Light*, in 2022. In connection with privacy, the FTC Staff Report identified dark patterns incorporated into user interfaces that:

paragraphs.text: (1) do not allow consumers to definitively reject data collection or use; (2) repeatedly prompt consumers to select settings they wish to avoid; (3) present confusing toggle settings leading consumers to make unintended privacy choices; (4) purposely obscure consumers’ privacy choices and make them difficult to access; (5) highlight a choice that results in more information collection, while greying out the option that enables consumers to limit such practices; and (6) include default settings that maximize data collection and sharing.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
SPECIFIC COMMENTS AND RECOMMENDATIONS
I. Section 1: Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: aspects of dark patterns are already addressed in existing EU law and guidance.
*Guidelines 03/2022 on Deceptive Design Patterns in Social Media Platform Interfaces: How to Recognise and Avoid Them*

paragraphs.text: As it develops the Digital Fairness Act, the Sections suggest that the Commission considers the extent to which aspects of dark patterns are already addressed in existing EU law and guidance. In the case of privacy, for example, the application of the GDPR to dark patterns was addressed by the European Data Protection Board in *Guidelines 03/2022 on Deceptive Design Patterns in Social Media Platform Interfaces: How to Recognise and Avoid Them*. We note that some dark patterns may inherently fall short of the GDPR’s requirements for consent (where consent is relied upon as the lawful basis of processing) and principles of lawfulness, fairness and transparency, data minimization, and purpose limitation. In particular:

I. Section 1: Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: not qualify as consent that is “freely given, . . . informed and unambiguous indication of the data subject’s wishes.”

paragraphs.text: The provision of personal data by an individual as a result of being fatigued, confused or otherwise misled by data patterns should typically not qualify as consent that is “freely given, . . . informed and unambiguous indication of the data subject’s wishes.”¹¹

paragraphs.text: Such circumstances may also result in noncompliance with the GDPR’s requirements that personal data be “collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes”¹³ and be “limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes”¹⁴ disclosed to the consumer.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
SPECIFIC COMMENTS AND RECOMMENDATIONS
I. Section 1: Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns to persuade or trick consumers into providing personal data that they would not otherwise provide,

paragraphs.text: To the extent that the Commission is concerned about use of dark patterns to persuade or trick consumers into providing personal data that they would not otherwise provide, the Sections suggest that the Commission consider whether more effective enforcement of these existing provisions would suffice. If the proposed Digital Fairness Act results in new provisions that overlap with existing provisions of the GDPR, we would encourage the Commission to provide guidance on the interplay between them. In doing so, we would also encourage the Commission to describe how coordination and coherence among authorities’ enforcement efforts will be ensured at both the regional and national levels.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
SPECIFIC COMMENTS AND RECOMMENDATIONS
I. Section 1: Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: We recognize that there are gaps between some of these laws. For instance, only a few gatekeepers will be caught by the Digital Markets Act. Nevertheless, altogether, these amount to an extensive body of law addressing dark patterns. We encourage the Commission to weigh carefully the benefits and drawbacks of introducing additional legislation as opposed to more vigorous enforcement and, if necessary, amendment of existing legislation, taking into account the already extensive range of digital legislation introduced over recent years.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
SPECIFIC COMMENTS AND RECOMMENDATIONS
I. Section 1: Dark Patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design in the online context
gaming

paragraphs.text: The Sections welcome cautious attention to addictive design in the online context but encourage thoughtful consideration of what elements should be taken into account in the scope of any prohibition. Many features of online interfaces today seek to attract the attention of users (e.g., social media content and gaming) and much of the internet is funded by these means. There is evidently high demand for entertaining content and services.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
II. Section 2: Addictive Design

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: There is merit to an approach to addictive design that considers the context and purpose of the design, and its effect on users, focused on specific harms and not broad-brush prohibitions that neglect the agency and economic freedom of individuals in choosing how to spend their time and money. As with dark patterns, we encourage the Commission to focus on substantial consumer injury that is not reasonably avoidable and is not outweighed by offsetting consumer or competitive benefits. The Commission should focus in particular on risk of harms to minors, who may be particularly vulnerable to risk of addiction both due to their developmental phase (e.g., lack of impulse control) and the increasing role that online activity has on the personal life of those in younger generations.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
II. Section 2: Addictive Design

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design
data about individuals is used to tailor the form of content and interfaces presented to them

paragraphs.text: Important privacy concerns arise in relation to addictive design where data about individuals is used to tailor the form of content and interfaces presented to them in a manner that exploits vulnerabilities. Generally, where personal data collected reveals (directly or by inference) that an individual is a child or may have particular vulnerabilities that could be exploited through addictive design (e.g., a history of gambling), then use of such data to promote services or content with addictive features will often be unfair. The Commission should be attentive to whether the provisions of the GDPR, the AI Act and the other laws mentioned above are already sufficient to prohibit use of such data in this manner.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
II. Section 2: Addictive Design

evidence_quotes.quote: The design of addictive interfaces can be highly effective and harmful even without exploiting personal data

paragraphs.text: The design of addictive interfaces can be highly effective and harmful even without exploiting personal data, and these may justify regulation quite apart from privacy considerations. This will be so particularly in relation to the protection of mental and physical health of, as well as risk of economic harm to, vulnerable consumers like children. These concerns are shared in the United States. Multiple U.S. state Attorneys General have brought cases alleging collection of

Addictive features that significantly increase minors’ exposure to specific harmful online content may include, for example, pornography, adult connections that could lead to sexual exploitation, content about unlawful drugs and cyberbullying.

paragraphs.text: large volumes of personal data of minors to train algorithms to induce children to keep using platforms, including through psychologically and emotionally distressing content. Addictive features that significantly increase minors’ exposure to specific harmful online content may include, for example, pornography, adult connections that could lead to sexual exploitation, content about unlawful drugs and cyberbullying.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
II. Section 2: Addictive Design

evidence_quotes.quote: Addictive design that negatively influences consumers’ transactional decisions may already amount to unfair commercial practice under the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, for example.
many of the comments above relating to dark patterns also apply.

paragraphs.text: The Sections encourage the Commission to ensure that protections against such harms are provided to minors irrespective of privacy considerations. It should, however, take into account whether these concerns are already addressed in existing legislation. Addictive design that negatively influences consumers’ transactional decisions may already amount to unfair commercial practice under the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, for example. To the extent that addictive design has the effect of reducing users’ ability to make free and informed decisions about their online behavior, many of the comments above relating to dark patterns also apply.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
II. Section 2: Addictive Design

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalization inherently involves use of personal data about individuals, whether for targeted advertising, customer-specific pricing, rankings or recommendations focused on preferences and behavior.
The Consumer Rights Directive requires disclosure of personalized pricing.

paragraphs.text: Personalization inherently involves use of personal data about individuals, whether for targeted advertising, customer-specific pricing, rankings or recommendations focused on preferences and behavior. As with the other areas considered for the proposed Digital Fairness Act, the Sections note that personalization practices are significantly addressed other law. The GDPR addresses fairness and transparency and automated decision-making and profiling. The Digital Services Act prohibits personalized advertising to minors relying on special categories of personal data. The Artificial Intelligence Act prohibits certain techniques exploiting vulnerabilities due to a person’s age, disability or a specific social or economic situation. And the Consumer Rights Directive requires disclosure of personalized pricing.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
III. Section 4: Unfair Personalization Practices

evidence_quotes.quote: Privacy concerns are significantly more acute where personal data may be used to exploit particular vulnerabilities.

paragraphs.text: Privacy concerns are significantly more acute where personal data may be used to exploit particular vulnerabilities. In the case of children, the Commission may wish to note recent

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
III. Section 4: Unfair Personalization Practices

evidence_quotes.quote: targeted advertising or commercial profiling purposes
personalization practices that unfairly take advantage of other vulnerabilities

paragraphs.text: amendments made to the U.S. Child Online Privacy Protection Rule (COPPA). This amended rule now requires operators of websites and online services to obtain separate verifiable parental consent before disclosing a child’s personal information to third parties for targeted advertising or commercial profiling purposes. The Sections would also welcome proposed prohibition of personalization practices that unfairly take advantage of other vulnerabilities, such as older age, particular medical conditions, addiction, gambling history, financial problems, lack of impulse control, or negative mental states.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
III. Section 4: Unfair Personalization Practices

evidence_quotes.quote: advertising based on profiling using personal data of children.
However, not all online personalization practices rely on AI.

paragraphs.text: Again, however, the Commission should consider carefully the extent to which existing legislation already addresses risks to persons with such vulnerabilities. For example, the EU’s Digital Services Act requires online platforms to put in place measures providing high levels of privacy, safety, and security of minors and prohibits online platforms from advertising based on profiling using personal data of children. However, these apply to online platforms but not universally to other digital service providers. The AI Act prohibits purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques or exploiting vulnerabilities. However, not all online personalization practices rely on AI. There does appear to be merit in addressing such gaps expressly. Nevertheless, the Sections suggest that Commission consider whether it is better to expand the application of existing legislation as an alternative to introducing an entirely new law. It should also consider any new prohibitions on personalization practices leading to specific harms after taking into consideration potential offsetting consumer and competitive benefits.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
III. Section 4: Unfair Personalization Practices

evidence_quotes.quote: “advertising and promotional messages that are not identifiable as advertising to consumers are deceptive if they mislead consumers into believing [the message is] independent, impartial, or not from the sponsoring advertiser itself.”³⁰

paragraphs.text: The U.S. approach to regulating influencer marketing has long roots in the FTC’s focus on ensuring that consumers understand when they are engaging with advertising. The FTC takes the view in its *Enforcement Policy Statement on Deceptively Formatted Advertisements* that “advertising and promotional messages that are not identifiable as advertising to consumers are deceptive if they mislead consumers into believing [the message is] independent, impartial, or not from the sponsoring advertiser itself.”³⁰ In other words, knowing the source of an advertisement typically affects the weight or credibility consumers give it and may affect the extent to which consumers choose to interact with content containing advertising at all.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT

paragraphs.text: In its *Guides Concerning the Use of Endorsements and Testimonials in Advertising*, the FTC makes clear that all parties participating in the influencer ecosystem share responsibility for any deceptive practices, including failure to make a material connection between an influencer and

IV. Section 5: Unfair Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: that have a role in creating or disseminating endorsements they knew or should have known are deceptive.
influencers disclose advertising clearly and conspicuously.
The Sections further encourage the Commission to take actions to encourage brands and agencies to take measures to ensure that influencers comply with legal obligations.

paragraphs.text: a marketer known. This includes responsibility for advertisers and influencer endorsers, as well as intermediaries, such as advertising agencies and public relations firms, that have a role in creating or disseminating endorsements they knew or should have known are deceptive. The Sections endorse actions to ensure that influencers disclose advertising clearly and conspicuously. The Sections further encourage the Commission to take actions to encourage brands and agencies to take measures to ensure that influencers comply with legal obligations.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
IV. Section 5: Unfair Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: V. Section 6: Pricing

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
IV. Section 5: Unfair Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: “drip pricing.”

paragraphs.text: The Sections support transparent pricing and efforts to reduce consumer confusion that may be caused by “drip pricing.” In the U.S., the FTC Junk Fees Rule, along with similar state laws, require that advertised or displayed prices reflect the total amount a consumer will pay at the point of sale with the exception of taxes and fees for optional services. These rules are designed to help consumers compare prices across sellers, reduce search costs, and promote fair competition among businesses competing on price.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
IV. Section 5: Unfair Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: drip pricing

paragraphs.text: As a federal regulation, the FTC’s Junk Fees Rule establishes a baseline standard; however, state laws differ in scope, definitions, and exceptions. While currently only a few states have enacted statutes that directly target drip pricing, approximately twenty other states are considering their own regulations. The variation between federal and state laws, as well as among the states, may create uncertainty regarding legal requirements and increase the cost of compliance for digital platforms and businesses that operate nationally. However, the Sections also recognize that state-level enforcement is an important tool for protecting consumers and ensuring that local markets adhere to fair pricing practices. The Sections therefore encourage the Commission to consider measures that balance consumer welfare, compliance burdens, and the important role of Member State enforcement.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
IV. Section 5: Unfair Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: VI. Section 7: Digital Contracts

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
IV. Section 5: Unfair Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: affirmative consent to subscriptions at the end of trial periods.

paragraphs.text: In relation to subscription contracts, the Sections encourage the Commission to focus on requirements for clear initial notice and consent, a proportionately easy method of cancellation, and affirmative consent to subscriptions at the end of trial periods. This is the framework the U.S. has adopted in its Restore Online Shopper’s Confidence Act (ROSCA), which has worked well to balance the pro-consumer benefits of offering ongoing subscription programs with protections to limit the potential for abuse. These rules would address broadly harmful, and well understood, practices.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
IV. Section 5: Unfair Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: prescriptive limitations on contracting such as mandating

paragraphs.text: To the extent a customer is engaging with an automated chatbot, the Sections encourage transparency. The Sections discourage prescriptive limitations on contracting such as mandating

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
IV. Section 5: Unfair Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: short notice cancellation

paragraphs.text: short notice cancellation as such limitations could raise costs for consumers and limit pro-consumer benefits.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
IV. Section 5: Unfair Practices by Social Media Influencers

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: The Commission’s concerns relating to digital contracts raise issues similar to those relating to dark patterns discussed above. According to the Commission’s Fitness Check, a significant number of people reported harm to their privacy because they unintentionally agreed to share more personal data than intended. Again, the Sections recommend that the Commission consider whether, at least with respect to privacy, more effective enforcement of existing provisions of the GDPR, Unfair Commercial Practice Directive, and Consumer Rights Directive sufficiently address the concern.

section_path: COMMENTS OF THE AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION ANTITRUST LAW SECTION AND INTERNATIONAL LAW SECTION ON THE CONSULTATION OF THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT IMPACT ASSESSMENT
IV. Section 5: Unfair Practices by Social Media Influencers

paragraphs.text: The Sections appreciate the opportunity to comment and remain available to respond to any questions regarding these comments or to provide additional assistance to the Commission as it may deem appropriate and helpful.
